text: 1 1950s-1980s: Rule-Based MT Hand-crafted grammars and bilingual dictionaries dominated the landscape 2 1990s-2000s: Statistical MT Data-driven approaches using parallel corpora and probability models emerged 3 Mid-2010s: Neural MT Deep learning revolutionized translation with sequence-to-sequence architectures 4

SMT marked a fundamental shift from human-crafted rules to learning from data. Instead of encoding linguistic knowledge manually, these systems extracted translation patterns from parallel corpora—large collections of documents professionally translated by humans. Sources included UN proceedings, Canadian Parliament records, and the European Parliament corpus, providing millions of sentence pairs for training.

SMT reframed translation as a decoding problem inspired by information theory. The model assumes the target language text was "corrupted" through a noisy channel into the source language. 1 Translation Model P(f|e) Ensures words are good matches for the source text 2 Language Model P(e) Ensures output sounds like natural human language 3
The encoder processes the source sentence sequentially, building up a representation that captures both the meaning of individual words and their relationships within the sentence.
The decoder generates the target sentence autoregressively—each word depends on all previously generated words. This creates fluent, coherent output that respects target language grammar.

Consider translating English "Red car" to French "Voiture rouge" (literally "Car red"). English places adjectives before nouns, while French places them after. 1 Generate "Voiture" Attention focuses strongly on "car" in the source 2 Generate "rouge" Attention shifts to focus on "red" in the source The model learns this reordering pattern automatically from data, without any hand-written rules about adjective placement.

Evaluating MT is notoriously difficult because there are many correct ways to translate any sentence. Automated metrics try to approximate human judgment by comparing machine output to reference translations. The industry standard is BLEU (Bilingual Evaluation Understudy), which measures n-gram overlap between candidate and reference translations.

Idioms present one of the most difficult challenges for MT systems. Consider the English idiom "kick the bucket," meaning to die. A literal translation to another language would describe striking a pail with one's foot—completely missing the figurative meaning. Neural models must learn which phrases are idiomatic and find equivalent expressions in the target language, not word-for-word translations.

European institutions, web crawls, and commercial sources 100K English-Swahili Limited parallel data available, primarily religious texts and news 1K English-Luganda Extremely limited resources, researchstage only Approaches for Low-Resource MT
TextRank applies PageRank-style graph algorithms to text. Each sentence becomes a node, and edges represent similarity between sentences based on word overlap.

stable The algorithm iteratively updates sentence scores until convergence, with important sentences being those highly connected to other important sentences—a recursive definition similar to PageRank for web pages.

Gain hands-on experience with modern neural machine translation systems through experimentation with pre-trained models and analysis of their behavior. 1 Back-Translation Experiment Use googletrans or Hugging Face MarianMT to translate complex English sentences to French, then translate back to English. Analyze meaning preservation and idiom handling. 2 Pre-trained Model Demo Load Helsinki-NLP/opus-mt-en-fr from Hugging Face and experiment with various sentence types. Document strengths and failure modes.

●Big Data refers to extremely large datasets that are difficult to process using traditional data processing applications and tools. In professional software development, code is read far more often than it is written. While a programmer may write a piece of code once, that same code may be read, reviewed, debugged, extended, or refactored hundreds of times by different people over several years. Clean code is therefore not a matter of personal style; it is a professional responsibility. “Any fool can write code that a computer can understand. Good programmers write code that humans can understand.” — Martin Fowler. What Is Clean Code?

Clean code communicates intent clearly, minimizes surprises, and reduces the cognitive effort required to understand how a system works. Why Clean Code Is a Core Part of Software Construction Clean code is central to software construction because software systems: Are built by teams, not individuals
Google maintains billions of lines of code across thousands of repositories. Engineers are often required to modify code written by someone they have never met.
summary: Industry Reality: Clean Code at Scale

text: Naming, structure, and clarity are prioritized over cleverness At Google, a change may be rejected not because it is incorrect, but because it is hard to read or understand.
Bugs to be fixed safely without introducing new ones Many system failures historically traced back to poorly structured, tightly coupled code, not lack of technical skill.

Real-World Example Consider a variable named x. What does x represent? Why does it exist? What unit or meaning does it carry? A descriptive name (e.g., totalInvoiceAmount) removes ambiguity and reduces errors.
A code smell is an indication that something may be wrong with the structure or design of the code. Code smells are not necessarily bugs, but they increase the risk of bugs.
summary: Code Smells: Warning Signs in Software

text: This week’s concepts connect directly to: Refactoring (Week 8) Testing (Week 7) Modular design (Week 5) Team development (Week 10) Clean code is not a one-time activity; it is a continuous practice throughout the software lifecycle.

<7> Put Batch Normalization into your architecture • Let's normalize all activations in a batch to have N(0,1) • Motivation : Internal Covariate Shift? – Distribution of activations at the 1st epoch is severely different from that of the 10th epo ch, even though inputs are same. – This is due to the change in parameters during training. – In other words, if we normalize batch activations, then parameters do not fluctuate, thus r esulting in fast & stable training. • E.g. BN-x5 needs 14 times fewer steps than Inception to reach the 72.2% accuracy. Ioffe, Sergey, and Christian Szegedy. "Batch normalization: Accelerating deep network training by reducing internal covariate shift." arXiv preprint arXiv:1 502.03167 (2015). 8

text: Digital marketing is the marketing done by digital technologies using the internet to market the products or services of a business and through any digital medium it is done. (or) The art of getting customers for a business via online is called digital marketing.

It helps you with personal branding. You can get a good job or become an entrepreneur. You can also help other companies as a consultant. You can use this knowledge to build your own business as marketing is the key for any business.

Digital marketing is a new field. Thousands of companies are looking for professionals in this field. There are no enough experts in the market. This is one of the highest paying jobs in the industry today. What to do if you are not interested to do job in Digital Marketing Start a side business and bring consistent income Video marketing, niche blogging etc. Consult clients on digital marketing strategies (charge per hour). Start a digital marketing agency. Who are eligible to learn Digital Marketing? Anyone can learn digital marketing. Subject of study doesn’t matter. Tech or No Tech background, Doesn't matter

Search engine marketing (SEM) is a form of internet advertising utilizing search engine results to promote brands and websites. It is a paid media where advertisers bid of specific search keywords that when typed in, their ads are shown. Below given image shows the paid ads on search engine.

PPC – Pay per click. CPC – Cost per click. CREATIVE – Heading and description of search ad. SERP – Search engine results page. CTR%– Click through rate (as a percentage). Impressions- The number of times your ad is shown. Google content Network– Display banner ads outside of google. Rank/position – The position your ad is shown when a keyword is searched. Quality Score– A ranking google gives your ad based on its CTR, relevance to the keyword and the landing page’s relevance and other factors.

Google dominates the search engine market share, averaging an 86–96% market share worldwide. Google's global search engine market share was 91.88% as of June 2022. Baidu reigns supreme in China with a 75.54% market share.

The marketing that is done on social media platform such as Facebook, twitter, Instagram, linked-in. Facebook has 2.91 billion as of 2023. The potential reach of Facebook Ads is 2.11 billion users According to Hootsuite, this reach is 72.5% of their total 2.91 billion monthly active users. The largest Facebook audience in 2021 is men aged between 25 and 34, accounting for 19% of total potential reach. From the above-mentioned point, you can just imagine how important (SMM) in digital marketing. It can create a great impact on getting huge number of audiences to engage with the content you put in various posts about your business. Now a days it’s very important to have social media presence for every business as many people are spending more time on Facebook, twitter etc. Facebook is the best platform for social media marketing as it is having more number of users. Cont.. Let’s take Facebook as our SMM platform and learn how to do marketing Below mentioned are the steps to be considered to marketing on Facebook. Setting up a Facebook page. scheduling the content and content distribution. Engaging with audiences on Facebook. Advertising on Facebook. Measuring the advertising campaigns through analytics. Setting up a Facebook page. If you have a personal account then you will be having a create Facebook page option. You can create a Facebook page on the name of your business. You can add an image as profile picture which is having your business logo. You can give all the details

Most people use social media to distract themselves from their regular work and relax. Entertainment need not necessarily be fun or humorous. It could also include facts; stories pictures etc. which people can relate to or get excited about according their interests. content distribution.

Millions of people post content on Facebook every day but only about 300 posts make it to each user’s news feed. In such a cut-throat competition, a good post is one which can grab the audiences “attention in the initial 2 or 3 seconds having an attention -grabbing headline is one such way. content distribution.

Providing only entertainment and no value to the audience may sometimes annoy them. It is thus judicious to combine entertainment with education. The content which receives high engagement rates even after long periods of posting it is known as evergreen content such content is highly valuable to a digital marketer. Such content may be posted multiple times to reach people in different time zones. Scheduling the content and content distribution. In content marketing you should post the content related to your business such as product information, useful tips, company news and photos and also company events which include staff. Scheduling the content in Facebook is very important. You should plan on what days in a week you distribute content so that you can post regularly according to the plan. While posting the content it is very important to give the URL of your ‘LANDING PAGE’ of your website .Landing page means the actual page of your website which your trying to promote or the page which consists of product purchase activities and important information or sign up form for a e-book or newsletter.

It doesn’t matter how much content you post on Facebook it matters when you with your audiences via replies and assisting their queries. Some users comment on post, ask questions and some other message on messenger and some like your posts. It’s your duty to say ‘THANK YOU’ if the post reaches highly and get huge response. Some email to the mail ID which you have provided on the page.

Email marketing is sending emails to your subscribers regarding your products, promotional offers and other information related your business. Email marketing is permission-based marketing. You cannot send an email without the person permission by just collecting data or email ids. If a person subscribes to your website or blog or for any other product then only you can send emails. Email marketing has automation capabilities. There are many automation tools for email marketing among them top tools are MAILCHIMP, DRIP and AWEBER. These email marketing tools will deliver your emails in bulk to all the subscribers.

Create a subscriber option in your blog or website for a product like e course, eBook, newsletter where customers enter their email id’s. Collect all the subscribers in the email marketing tool. So now all the email ids are in the tool. Email marketing tools has the automation techniques or the work flow designed by you as per the work flow the customers get emails in regular intervals. After a time gap the promotional mail will go to the customer according to the automated work flow. MEASURING THE EMAIL MARKETING CAMPAIGNS THROUGH ANALYTICS To measure the email marketing campaigns there is an analytics option in the tool where you can monitor. Metrics like CTR (Click through rate) and how many people have actually seen the email and how many people opened it are very useful insights to enhance your email marketing campaigns. Click through rate is the ratio of users who click on a link to the number of total users who view an email. For suppose 100 people viewed the email but only 20 people clicked on the link given in the email then the CTR is 20%. HOW EMAIL MARKETING IS USEFUL TO THE BUSINESS It gives valuable information to the customer through emails It increases sales in the business. It maintains relationship with the customers. Email marketing plays a major role in digital marketing. Marketers use it as a primary for business. Some business gets revenue through email marketing by increased sales. This is how the marketing through email works. HOW TO DO A/B TESTING IN E
summary: STEPS TO FOLLOW TO DO EMAIL MARKETING

text: Content marketing is defined as creating and distributing valuable and relevant content to attract the audiences. Content is in many forms. You can create and distribute content in the form of videos, images, blogging (blogs), eBooks, social media posts.

VIDEOS, IMAGES VIDEOS, IMAGES Video is one of the best forms that you can create content. You can create an exciting and engaging video related to your products and distribute it to blogs, social media etc. and market it to many targeted audiences.

Before you write content, you should know your niche(kingdom) especially which age they belong to. You should write content based on their interest, age, gender and many factors to consider. For instance, if you have a blog on muscle building or body building related topic. For those gym going youngsters are more interested. You can consider from age 20-35 years and write content which suits them. Likewise, for every topic you should write content based on segmentation which benefits your blog, eCommerce websites, social media pages etc. MEASURING SUCCESS IN CONTENT MARKETING THROUGH ANALYTICS You can measure your success in content marketing through analytics. For instance, if you write a blog post you can notice how many people read the post. Tools like google analytics for blog, Facebook analytics for social media posts. After getting insights you can improve your content marketing skills better.

Display advertising is defined as advertising on websites, mobile apps and social media through banners or the other ad format like images, text, video and audio. The main objective of display advertising is to promote the brand. In display advertising you should know who is an advertiser and who is a publisher

You are having a website and you want to display your ads on other websites to increase sales and traffic. Suppose you have film review blog and you want to increase your traffic through display advertising. First you need to make a list of publisher websites that you want to advertise on. In display advertising you can advertising on your product related websites like Book my show, PVR other movie ticket booking partners because your website or blog is related to films. Not only on product related websites but also you can advertise on websites where nothing is related. But first preference should be on related websites. Now you start looking for a display ad network where it shows some websites to advertise on and pay according to cost per click, cost per impressions. Thus, the display advertising is done.

First a visitor checks into your website for some product and leaves without making a purchase. After leaving your website visitor goes into other site then your banner appears on that website this is called re marketing in display advertising. Re marketing increases the sales and brand name. Re marketing brings customers back.

Conversion rate optimization is an ongoing process of increasing the percentage of visitors to your website who will take an action such as filling a contact form or purchasing a product on your site. In CRO you can understand the visitor’s journey on your site like how the visitor is moving on your site and what is that stopping the visitor to make an action.

You can improve the conversion rate by understanding your customers better. Take the insights from your website traffic data and improve the user experience on your site. Designing the beautiful landing pages where the customer lands to perform the action. Improve the conversion rate by giving offers to the customers. Promote your landing page with different social media channels and increase conversions and conversion rate.

You can improve the conversion rate by understanding your customers better. Take the insights from your website traffic data and improve the user experience on your site. Designing the beautiful landing pages where the customer lands to perform the action. Improve the conversion rate by giving offers to the customers. Tool that helps you to improve conversion rate. Lucky orange is a tool that gives you the total user experience on your site such as (recordings of your visitors on the site). Metrics are quantitative measurements. It’s a number (an average, Ratio) for example – How many visitors came to your website today is a metric. How many sessions happened today is also a metric? Dimensions are attributes of your data. Example- visitors they came from (location) is a dimension. They came from “pairs” (Paris is a dimension).

Mobile marketing is a digital marketing strategy to reach target audience on their smartphones, tablets through websites, emails, SMS and MMS, social media and apps. In earlier days people used to use desktops for opening an email to visiting a website and reading content. Now this can be done on a small screen mobile phone. 95% of the internet users own a smart phone now-a-day. According to the google more than 50% of search queries throughout the globe come from mobile devices. In united states mobile platforms such as smartphones and tablets, host up to 60% of digital media time for users.

Prediction. To be able to predict what the responses are going to be to future input variables; Information. To extract some information about how nature is associating the response variables to the input variables. There are two different approaches toward these goals:
summary: There are two goals in analyzing the data:

text: The analysis in this culture starts with assuming a stochastic data model for the inside of the black box. For example, a common data model is that data are generated by independent draws from response variables = f(predictor variables, random noise, parameters) Leo Breiman is Professor, Department of Statistics, University of California, Berkeley, California 947204735 (e-mail: leo@stat.berkeley.edu). The values of the parameters are estimated from the data and the model then used for information and/or prediction. Thus the black box is ﬁlled in like this: y x linear regression logistic regression

The analysis in this culture considers the inside of the box complex and unknown. Their approach is to ﬁnd a function fx—an algorithm that operates on x to predict the responses y. Their black box looks like this: y x unknown decision trees neural nets Model validation. Measured by predictive accuracy. Estimated culture population. 2% of statisticians, many in other ﬁelds. In this paper I will argue that the focus in the statistical community on data models has: • Led to irrelevant theory and questionable scientiﬁc conclusions; 199 200 L. BREIMAN • Kept statisticians from using more suitable algorithmic models; • Prevented statisticians from working on exciting new problems; I will also review some of the interesting new developments in algorithmic modeling in machine learning and look at applications to three data sets. 2. ROAD MAP It may be revealing to understand how I became a member of the small second culture. After a sevenyear stint as an academic probabilist, I resigned and went into full-time free-lance consulting. After thirteen years of consulting I joined the Berkeley Statistics Department in 1980 and have been there since. My experiences as a consultant formed my views about algorithmic modeling. Section 3 describes two of the projects I worked on. These are given to show how my views grew from such problems. When I returned to the university and began reading statistical journals, the research was distant from what I had done as a consultant. All articles begin

Section 11 is titled “Information from a Black Box” and is important in showing that an algorithmic model can produce more and more reliable information about the structure of the relationship between inputs and outputs than data models. This is illustrated using two medical data sets and a genetic data set. A glossary at the end of the paper explains terms that not all statisticians may be familiar with. 3. PROJECTS IN CONSULTING As a consultant I designed and helped supervise surveys for the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) and the state and federal court systems. Controlled experiments were designed for the EPA, and I analyzed trafﬁc data for the U.S. Department of Transportation and the California Transportation Department. Most of all, I worked on a diverse set of prediction projects. Here are some examples: Predicting next-day ozone levels. Using mass spectra to identify halogen-containing compounds. Predicting the class of a ship from high altitude radar returns. Using sonar returns to predict the class of a submarine. Identity of hand-sent Morse Code. Toxicity of chemicals. On-line prediction of the cause of a freeway trafﬁc breakdown.

201 daylight hour varying only a few percent from one weekday to another. With the total amount of emissions about constant, the resulting ozone levels depend on the meteorology of the preceding days. A large data base was assembled consisting of lower and upper air measurements at U.S. weather stations as far away as Oregon and Arizona, together with hourly readings of surface temperature, humidity, and wind speed at the dozens of air pollution stations in the Basin and nearby areas. Altogether, there were daily and hourly readings of over 450 meteorological variables for a period of seven years, with corresponding hourly values of ozone and other pollutants in the Basin. Let x be the predictor vector of meteorological variables on the nth day. There are more than 450 variables in x since information several days back is included. Let y be the ozone level on the n + 1st day. Then the problem was to construct a function fx such that for any future day and future predictor variables x for that day, fx is an accurate predictor of the next day’s ozone level y. To estimate predictive accuracy, the ﬁrst ﬁve years of data were used as the training set. The last two years were set aside as a test set. The algorithmic modeling methods available in the pre1980s decades seem primitive now. In this project large linear regressions were run, followed by variable selection. Quadratic terms in, and interactions among, the retained variables were added and variable selection used again to p

203 answer the question posed? Is inference justiﬁed when your sample is the entire population? Should a data model be used? The deﬁciencies in analysis occurred because the focus was on the model and not on the problem. The linear regression model led to many erroneous conclusions that appeared in journal articles waving the 5% signiﬁcance level without knowing whether the model ﬁt the data. Nowadays, I think most statisticians will agree that this is a suspect way to arrive at conclusions. At the time, there were few objections from the statistical profession about the fairy-tale aspect of the procedure, But, hidden in an elementary textbook, Mosteller and Tukey (1977) discuss many of the fallacies possible in regression and write “The whole area of guided regression is fraught with intellectual, statistical, computational, and subject matter difﬁculties.” Even currently, there are only rare published critiques of the uncritical use of data models. One of the few is David Freedman, who examines the use of regression models (1994); the use of path models (1987) and data modeling (1991, 1995). The analysis in these papers is incisive. 5.2 Problems in Current Data Modeling Current applied practice is to check the data model ﬁt using goodness-of-ﬁt tests and residual analysis. At one point, some years ago, I set up a simulated regression problem in seven dimensions with a controlled amount of nonlinearity. Standard tests of goodness-of-ﬁt did not reject linearity until the nonl

205 Perhaps the damaging consequence of the insistence on data models is that statisticians have ruled themselves out of some of the most interesting and challenging statistical problems that have arisen out of the rapidly increasing ability of computers to store and manipulate data. These problems are increasingly present in many ﬁelds, both scientiﬁc and commercial, and solutions are being found by nonstatisticians. 7. ALGORITHMIC MODELING Under other names, algorithmic modeling has been used by industrial statisticians for decades. See, for instance, the delightful book “Fitting Equations to Data” (Daniel and Wood, 1971). It has been used by psychometricians and social scientists. Reading a preprint of Giﬁ’s book (1990) many years ago uncovered a kindred spirit. It has made small inroads into the analysis of medical data starting with Richard Olshen’s work in the early 1980s. For further work, see Zhang and Singer (1999). Jerome Friedman and Grace Wahba have done pioneering work on the development of algorithmic methods. But the list of statisticians in the algorithmic modeling business is short, and applications to data are seldom seen in the journals. The development of algorithmic methods was taken up by a community outside statistics. 7.1 A New Research Community In the mid-1980s two powerful new algorithms for ﬁtting data became available: neural nets and decision trees. A new research community using these tools sprang up. Their goal was predictive accuracy. The comm

which four people, from different vantage points, witness an incident in which one person dies and another is supposedly raped. When they come to testify in court, they all report the same facts, but their stories of what happened are very different. What I call the Rashomon Effect is that there is often a multitude of different descriptions [equations fx] in a class of functions giving about the same minimum error rate. The most easily understood example is subset selection in linear regression. Suppose there are 30 variables and we want to ﬁnd the best ﬁve variable linear regressions. There are about 140,000 ﬁve-variable subsets in competition. Usually we pick the one with the lowest residual sum-of-squares (RSS), or, if there is a test set, the lowest test error. But there may be (and generally are) many ﬁve-variable equations that have RSS within 1.0% of the lowest RSS (see Breiman, 1996a). The same is true if test set error is being measured. So here are three possible pictures with RSS or test set error within 1.0% of each other:

213 the misclassiﬁcation error rate is 28%. The variable importance given by random forests is in Figure 4. Blood tests 3 and 5 are the most important, followed by test 4. Random forests also outputs an intrinsic similarity measure which can be used to cluster. When this was applied, two clusters were discovered in class two. The average of each variable is computed and plotted in each of these clusters in Figure 5. An interesting facet emerges. The class two subjects consist of two distinct groups: those that have high scores on blood tests 3, 4, and 5 and those that have low scores on those tests. 11.3 Example III: Microarray Data Random forests was run on a microarray lymphoma data set with three classes, sample size of 81 and 4,682 variables (genes) without any variable selection [for more information about this data set, see Dudoit, Fridlyand and Speed, (2000)]. The error rate was low. What was also interesting from a scientiﬁc viewpoint was an estimate of the importance of each of the 4,682 gene expressions. The graph in Figure 6 was produced by a run of random forests. This result is consistent with assessments of variable importance made using other algorithmic methods, but appears to have sharper detail. 11.4 Remarks about the Examples The examples show that much information is available from an algorithmic model. Friedman Fig. 6. Microarray variable importance. (1999) derives similar variable information from a different way of constructing a forest. The similarity

215 bagged predictor at x. In classiﬁcation, that class which has the plurality vote of the φkx is the bagged predictor. Bagging has been shown effective in variance reduction (Breiman, 1996b). Boosting. This is a more complex way of forming an ensemble of predictors in classiﬁcation than bagging (Freund and Schapire, 1996). It uses no randomization but proceeds by altering the weights on the training set. Its performance in terms of low prediction error is excellent (for details see Breiman, 1998).

formed in three decades of conversations with my old friend and collaborator, Jerome Friedman. Conversations with Richard Olshen about the Cox model and its use in biostatistics helped me to understand the background. I am also indebted to William Meisel, who headed some of the prediction projects I consulted on and helped me make the transition from probability theory to algorithms, and to Charles Stone for illuminating conversations about the nature of statistics and science. I’m grateful also for the comments of the editor, Leon Gleser, which prompted a major rewrite of the ﬁrst draft of this manuscript and resulted in a different and better paper.
